item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't an SCP. It's a tale. It's a good tale, but it is definitely a tale and belongs over with the rest of the tales rather than where it's currently at.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would be remiss (or at least out of character) if I didn't point out that I did it first. The stories do focus on largely different things even if they have a few shared trappings though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While this is wonderfully written and really engaging, i just want to remark on this SCP's little resemblance to the Tanhony's proposal, in the sense that the Ethics Commitee and the O5 Council are again warring against each other.

Having said that, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's the new bosses, same as the old bosses.

As for the timeline, all of the relevant articles and tales to this story are set in a fairly near future. There's no particular reason for that beyond my own preferences and the positioning of a few significant geopolitical events.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is awesome. Upvoted with all my heart. Viva la revolución of the Ethics Commitee.
I have one question, though, why the need to make it 2066? Is it just not to mess with the reader's current headcanon, if they believe the O5 Council to currently be good people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was my theory was well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wouldn't it anomalously influencing everyone be kind of cliche? I feel like I'd be rolling my eyes at that one. I'm generally unwilling to make this article any longer even to add additional context, but I would still argue that the Ethics Committees actions are reasonable here given the details I provided.

+ show block
– hide block

The Overseer Council is preventing them from carrying out their duties in this case and is unwilling to change course. No surprise there, especially given how antagonistic their communications with each other are. Probably some bad blood there. The Overseers themselves are also unpopular among certain levels of leadership (as is implicitly noted in the event timeline.) Given that Overseers aren't typically portrayed as being democratically elected, there aren't a whole lot of institutional options that come to mind for getting rid of them. Maybe some sort of broader vote of no confidence, but I think that's a different story entirely. The Ethics Committee could also roll over and given up on this specific issue. Again, a different story, but in particular this would establish an unpleasant precedent and might itself be violating some institutional norms.

Without a good way to replace Overseers from outside the council, and assuming that neither side plans to give up, resorting to violence is not unreasonable. The Overseers have a capacity for "disappearing" people (see: Alpha-1 or other internal security measures,) meaning that delaying action may have negative consequences for the Committee members involved. Given Sakarya's presented history of trouble, this probably isn't all that unlikely. If they're taking action first, decapitating leadership (or at least escalating in an attempt to prevent even further escalation) would both hinder retaliation and possibly end the conflict immediately. Killing one Overseer probably isn't worth it overall unless you were entirely sure that they were specifically the problem and that you could entirely avoid attribution after the fact, so a bunch of them have to go.

All that's especially true given the apparent loophole in the cited empty chairs clause that allows for the formation of a new Overseer Council even if they were the ones to break up the old one. That's a nice bonus. Too bad none of it went cleanly here.

It's the fault of my writing if that doesn't come across in the article without my explanation, but I don't think anything presented there is unreasonable for clandestine organization with lots of guns and inherently antagonistic power structures.

EDIT: All that said, I ended up making another minor addition to the intelligence brief regarding Sakarya's motivations and character.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really want to like this, but I have to second others about the Ethics Committee's actions being absurd and out-of-proportion. I thought as I read that the twist was going to be that the skip itself is anomalously inducing conflict, but no, they're really just acting like that. I can't get on board there.

item.title: Re: Admitted idiot
item.content:encoded: Not to be too self indulgent, but the best hints are probably in the two articles I wrote before this one: SCP-910 and SCP-3791. Gotta keep my internal continuity going.

item.title: Admitted idiot
item.content:encoded:
So I get that the whole point of this skip is that feuds between powerful individuls can spiral into organization destroying proxy wars but you spent so much time dangling a juicy skip in front of our faces that I just need to know

Is there something under that black box and if so can someone give me a hint?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad to see people coming down on both sides of the interpretation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …or else the new ones are equally wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Brilliant. All that strife and destruction, only to find out the original O5s were right all along.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Welp here we go again *insert to be continued meme*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's nice to break from the untouchable O5 trope. Also, after reading other people's headcanons, I came to the conclusion that it doesn't really matter what the SCP initially is. Site-262 is just a great way to get rid of some unwanted personnel since it's already very isolated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
expanding on your HC
– hide block

I think the original SCP-3985 is The Administrator. There was a rebellion/coup which led to the (now former) O5 council. Based on the limited description available, The Administrator had augmented themselves with anomalous objects/SCPs and is/was probably immortal/unkillable. The necessity for a null personality is also interesting, as it implies that should SCP-3985 regain a sense of self, they would be capable of regaining control of the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After reading the proposed documentation revision, my tentative
headcanon - 
headcanon

is that the current 3985 is the former O5s, and the former 3985 was one of their predecessors who went rogue. Admittedly with less success than the chairwoman. But this

- can't be proven. So I admire this for looping in a massive internecine Foundation conflict without definitively spoiling the anomaly's mystery!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a fun read. I admit that I hope we'll someday get a spoiler as to what the actual SCP is, but I realize that that's far from the actual point of the article, hah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yea I saw it, I was just too bored with the concept at that point for it to have any effect on me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I disagree with the idea that the chairwoman's a self-insert (an OC does not a self-insert make), but otherwise, well, that's kind of the point. The SCP itself (so far as I can tell?) is supposed to be insignificant and not have any traits besides being a humanoid-rights concern. The meat of the article's in the "killing the whole O5 Council".

Did you miss the (spoiler alert) whammy at the end about the one entity now being fourteen?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I'm not quite seeing how it's a self-insert.
btw +1 nice work Pedantique

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I’m curious what about Elif gives the impression that she's a self-insert.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Idk. It feels like it's just a tale where a self-insert character kills the whole O5 council instead of being an actual scp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hint: There was only one humanoid in containment before. At the end there are fourteen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for pointing that error out for me. Something always manages to slip through.

I’m not going to comment on the anomaly itself because I’ve been enjoying reading people’s theories about it, and I don’t think it’s particularly important to what I had intended the article to be about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Incident involving Site-106 internal security culminating in the death of 26 research research.

26 whole research? Egad! (Might want to fix this one :p)

Also, I'm going out on a limb and saying the "14 humanoids" are the ex-councilmen and ex-councilwomen, in addition to the original SCP-3985. But is there any significance to the original 3985? Is it meant to be a political prisoner, or something the O5 Council have reason to keep under wraps, or is it just a generic high-value SCP? The O5's keeping it under wraps for seemingly no reason is the crux of the article, so unless they've got good reason to hide it, the civil war seems kind of pointless

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But just what IS it? it looks like sakurya managed to get into the council, and now she agrees that the information must be kept confidential, so what the hell is it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well written but the contents are unsubtle and predictable.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty much how I imagine the O5's relationship with the rest of the Foundation, based on my understanding of how complex organizations work - especially ones where day-to-day business involves making decisions that affect people's lives.

There's an official hierarchy to every organization, but as things get larger they have to decentralize, so enclaves form, and then power and influence get redistributed little by little. Eventually the authority of the leaders effectively becomes limited to courses of action they can get a majority of their subordinates to approve of.

That's usually about the time everything falls apart.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, those are some fair points. What I meant specifically was, if one of the O5 says that some document is going to be heavily redacted or whatever, or something is or isn't going to happen, that's more or less the way it's going to go. Still, setting things a significant distance in the future as your article is can get around a lot of personnel canon conflicts like that, so it's not a huge issue.

My head canon is such that, while people will be people as you said, if someone was going to kick up that much of a fuss about it to actually cause problems, they would indeed find themselves in different employment, but it wouldn't be their choice and they wouldn't remember ever working for the Foundation after the fact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll add a line or two in the intelligence memo to suggest a broader history of conflict/escalation since that's clearly been a point of concern with multiple people.

As to the point about O5s having absolute power over the Foundation, I think that would be extremely infeasible in any iteration of the organization that wasn't extremely small. Even if they intend to execute unilateral authority, they need to delegate to accomplish complex tasks (especially when they involve incredibly specific fields of research taking place in locations across the world.) That means the creation of power centers that may prioritize their interests differently and may be willing to make life difficult for their bosses. This is particularly difficult in membership-voluntary organizations, as preserving employee morale and satisfaction is necessary to maintain a continued workforce, especially one consisting of very smart people who could probably find jobs elsewhere (protecting reality is important, but at the end of the day people are still going to be people.)

All of that is to say that O5s probably love to think that they can order everyone else around, but I don't think the Foundation could function without other powerful bodies forming and eventually coming into some mode of conflict with them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My issue with this is that, as said by others, the response of the ethics committee seems extreme to the point of laughability. I was waiting for the reveal that 3985 was something that caused them to react in such an extreme to a lack of information about it, or something like that, and it never came.

Maybe if there was some more indication that in 2066 the entire Foundation had become massively guided by the ethics committee to the point that some redacted text like this would be unthinkable (which would be a stretch given the redacted info in other articles), or some indication that the O5 Council had become dictatorial tyrants as the committee chair tries to imply, it might have landed a bit more. (Although for the record, I think the O5 Council is MEANT to have absolute control over everything like that anyway.)

As it stands, I feel that "thing that is so bad/scary/whatever that we can't possibly even let anyone know about it" has been done many times before.

Decent enough and well written though, and I did like the timeline of events, so I'll leave it at no-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was my eventual interpretation, though I was also was like "Whoa, hostile takeover for one black-box?" at first. You might want to make that idea a little more explicit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Concurring with Erasmus:

The Ethics Committee's actions also felt wildly disproportionate to the point that it stretched my suspension of disbelief, as did the O5s and their supporters being defeated almost effortlessly.

I feel as though there is a way around this that's pretty much telegraphed, though. You're setting up right from the initial exchanges that the Ethics Committee has information on pretty much every procedure the Foundation has. Get enough analysis going and you can probably ferret out the routines of the 05s from deviations in the procedures.

Not a perfect piece in detail, but a good narrative arc and an interesting take on the format seems worth a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Btw, this should have the Ethics Comittee tag.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I can see the similarities between this and the articles Erasmus linked, I think this explored quite a different approach that made it stand out and enjoyable. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I imagine their actions feel more proportional in the context of a long series of humiliations and general undermining, which has always seemed to me to be the most realistic relationship that could be had between the two. I would also quibble with the effortless description, but it is what it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If 231 and Ecce Perago didn't already exist this could be a classic, but as both of those are both existent and venerated, I can't really sink my teeth into this. It feels like it goes halfway in both those directions instead of going all the way in its own direction. The Ethics Committee's actions also felt wildly disproportionate to the point that it stretched my suspension of disbelief, as did the O5s and their supporters being defeated almost effortlessly. The setup and payoff with the two email exchanges was quite nice though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first I was like "This is basically SCP-579" then I was like "Oh this is actually pretty good" and then I was like "Upvoted"

item.title: Author Note
item.content:encoded:
People shape bureaucracies. Bureaucracies shape people. Individuals view their own work as of prime importance, necessitating the continuation of the bureaucracy as the means by which important work is done. Also, missiles.

More writing can be found at my author page.

Signature images were generated with this website.

(Also thanks to fieldstone for giving this a look when it was a draft. Thanks to DrMagnus for coding help.)
